coredata.dc:title: Non-toxic chitosan-based hydrogel with strong adsorption and sensitive detection abilities for tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Qiuyan
Ren, Tingting
Lei, Zihua
Huang, Yifeng
Huang, Yong
Xu, Dong
Wan, Caichao
Guo, Xin
Wu, Yiqiang
coredata.dc:description: Antibiotics are classified as a severe cause of water contamination. Therefore, there is a great demand for an efficient and non-toxic adsorbent for antibiotics. In this study, a non-toxic chitosan-based hydrogel (CH) was developed using cellulose nanocrystals (CNs) and carbon dots (CDs). This CH had strong sorption ability (a maximum sorption capacity of 541.3 mg/g) and sensitive detection ability (linear ranges of 0.2–1.0 μg/L and 1–200 mg/L, and a detection limit of 0.12 μg/L) for tetracycline (TC). The physical and chemical structures, cytotoxicity, TC sorption and detection abilities of the CH were studied. Sorption and detection mechanisms were proposed. The study revealed that CNs offered a skeleton structure for improving the adsorption ability, and the CDs served as remarkable fluorescent probes for detecting TC with high selectivity and sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Carbon dots
Cellulose nanocrystals
Adsorption
Tetracycline

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Hunan Youth Science and Technology Innovation Talent Project (Grant No. 2020RC3043), Training Plan of Young Backbone Teachers in Colleges and Universities of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 71), Special Projects of Scientific and Technological Innovation in Hunan Forestry, China (Grant No. XLK201982), Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project of Hunan Province (Grant No. 50), and Key Support Areas of Hunan College Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (Grant No. 20).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of CH fabrication.

(a) Photographs of the prepared CHs under daylight and UV light. (b–c) SEM and optical microscopy images. (d) XRD spectra. (e) FTIR spectra. (f) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. (g) Excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviour.

Effects of (a) pH and (b) temperature on the sorption of TC (contact time: 2 d; concentration: 40 mg/L; adsorbent dosage: 3.0 g). (c) Sorption isotherms (temperature: 298 K; pH: 8; contact time: 2 d; adsorbent dosage: 3.0 g). (d) Adsorption kinetics (temperature: 298 K; pH: 8; initial concentration: 40 mg/L; adsorbent dosage: 3.0 g). (e–f) Experimental data fitted by Langmuir and Freundlich models. (g–h) Experimental data fitted by the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models.
Fluorescence quenching (a) and AQY (b) of the CH-8 in the presence of different antibiotics (concentration: 2000 mg/L; pH: 8; quenching time: 5 min; temperature: 298 K).

(a) Fluorescence quenching and AQY of CH-8 for different TC concentrations (pH: 8; quenching time: 5 min; temperature: 298 K). (b) Fluorescent spectra of CH-8 for different TC concentrations and plots of the values of F0/F–1 versus concentration (pH: 8; quenching time: 5 min; temperature: 298 K).
(a) Cell viability. (b) Fluorescent images of L929 cells.
(a) FTIR spectra before and after TC sorption, (b) Full XPS spectra of CH-8, (c-d) O1s and N1s.

(a) UV-vis absorption spectrum of TC and excitation spectrum of CH-8. (b) Fluorescence decay curves of CH-8 and CH-8 with adsorbed TC. (c–d) Fluorescence decay curves fitted by second-order exponential functions.
Maximum sorption capacities in comparison with previous work.

row.entry: Glutaraldehyde-crosslinked electrospun Nanofibers of chitosan/poly (vinyl alcohol)

head.ce:title: Non-toxic chitosan-based hydrogel with strong adsorption and sensitive detection abilities for tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibiotics are classified as a severe cause of water contamination. Therefore, there is a great demand for an efficient and non-toxic adsorbent for antibiotics. In this study, a non-toxic chitosan-based hydrogel (CH) was developed using cellulose nanocrystals (CNs) and carbon dots (CDs). This CH had strong sorption ability (a maximum sorption capacity of 541.3 mg/g) and sensitive detection ability (linear ranges of 0.2–1.0 μg/L and 1–200 mg/L, and a detection limit of 0.12 μg/L) for tetracycline (TC). The physical and chemical structures, cytotoxicity, TC sorption and detection abilities of the CH were studied. Sorption and detection mechanisms were proposed. The study revealed that CNs offered a skeleton structure for improving the adsorption ability, and the CDs served as remarkable fluorescent probes for detecting TC with high selectivity and sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are a class of emerging contaminants. Antibiotic contamination can cause an increase in resistant microbes and genes and potentially threaten ecosystem functions and human health. Several treatment methods for removing antibiotics, such as ozonation [1], microbial degradation [2], membrane separation [3], photocatalytic degradation [4], and adsorption [5], have been established. Among these methods, adsorption is a practical approach. For achieving greater success with this approach, it is essential to explore novel adsorbents with excellent adsorption properties.

Chitosan (CS) is a natural biological polysaccharide and is known as a promising polymer for developing novel adsorbents. Ranjbari et al. prepared ionic liquid-impregnated chitosan hydrogel for removing tetracycline (TC), and this hydrogel had the maximum adsorption capacity of 22.42 mg/g [6]. Afzal et al. synthesised chitosan/biochar hydrogel to remove ciprofloxacin, revealed the active sites for ciprofloxacin sorption and examined the sorption mechanism [7]. Wang et al. synthesised a granular chitosan-based hydrogel to remove ciprofloxacin and enrofloxacin, and measured the sorption capacity of the hydrogel [8]. These chitosan-based hydrogels show great potential for removing antibiotics, and various methods such as blending and crosslinking with other preferable nanomaterials are adopted to further enhance their adsorption performance. Among these nanomaterials, cellulose nanocrystals (CNs) have excellent physical, chemical and biological properties, such as good thermal stability, high strength, high aspect ratio and non-toxicity [9]. Moreover, the CNs can offer a natural skeleton for assembling three-dimensional (3D) porous structures and provide ample hydroxyl groups for facilitating the accumulation and adsorption of antibiotics [10]. Hence, CNs are a promising candidate for improving the adsorbent.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of zero-dimensional nanomaterials that are emerging as functional fluorescent probes [11]. They possess highly favourable properties such as fluorescence, non-toxicity and biocompatibility. In particular, many reports reveal that they can be potentially used for analysing TC [12,13]. However, CDs can agglomerate easily owing to their high specific surface area, causing much inconvenience during practical use. To solve this problem, CDs can be immobilised into a stable matrix (e.g. a chitosan-based hydrogel).

Against this background, this work aimed to develop a non-toxic chitosan-based hydrogel (CH) with strong adsorption and sensitive detection abilities for TC using CS, CNs and CDs. The physical and chemical structures, cytotoxicity, TC sorption and detection abilities of the developed CH were investigated. Moreover, the adsorption and detection mechanisms of the CH were proposed.

2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO, 98%), citric acid (CA), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), chitosan (CS), acetic acid (AA), glutaric dialdehyde (GD), tetracycline (TC), ofloxacin (OFX), oxytetracycline (OTC), chloramphenicol (CAP), sulfadiazine (SD), sulfamethazine (SMZ), ciprofloxacin (CIP), and enrofloxacin (ENR) were purchased from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. (China).

The CNs preparation procedures were similar to those reported in the literature [14]. Dried bleached Eucalyptus urophylla pulp (10 g) was suspended in 400 mL of distilled water. Subsequently, 0.08 g of TEMPO, 0.4 g of NaBr and 60 g of NaClO solution (10 wt%) were mixed under continuous stirring. Meanwhile, the pH of the mixture was monitored and constantly adjusted to 10–11. After stirring for 6 h, oxidation was terminated by adding 60 mL of ethanol. The oxidised mixture was washed with distilled water until a neutral pH solution was obtained. Then, the mixture was put into a high-pressure homogeniser and homogenised five times. After homogenisation, a CNs suspension (1.0 wt%) was obtained.

The CDs were synthesised by the hydrothermal method [15]. First, 1.1 g of citric acid and 335 μL of ethylamine were introduced into 10 mL of distilled water. Subsequently, this reaction solution was placed in a Teflon-sealed autoclave. After maintaining at 473 K for 5 h, it was cooled to 298 K. Then, the synthesised CDs were subjected to dialysis against distilled water for 2 d and then stored in dark at 277 K for further application. The morphology and particle size distributions of the prepared CDs were investigated by using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Fig. S1).

First, 6.0 g of CNs suspension (1.0 wt%) was suspended in 3.0 mL of acetate buffer solution (PH = 3.5). Thereafter, 15 mg of EDC and 60 mg of NHS were added and mixed uniformly. Then, 1.0 g of CDs solution with different concentrations (0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.3 wt%, 0.4 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 0.6 wt% and 0.7 wt%) was added, respectively. Under continuous stirring, the reaction lasted overnight at 298 K and protected from light. The target product was subjected to dialysis for 4 days and then labelled as CNs-CDs-1, CNs-CDs-2, CNs-CDs-3, CNs-CDs-4, CNs-CDs-5, CNs-CDs-6 and CNs-CDs-7. As the content ratio of CNs and CDs is an important factor that determines the fluorescent behaviour, it was further optimised by fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S2, the CNs-CDs-6 exhibited the strongest fluorescent intensity. Hence, the optimal concentration of CDs was identified to be 0.6 wt%. Using the optimised experimental condition, the CNs-CDs suspension was synthesised for further study.

For fabricating the CH, 10 mL of CNs-CDs suspension was mixed well with 20 mL of AA solution (2.0 wt%) under magnetic stirring, and 1.5 g of CS powder was dissolved in 73.5 g of AA solution (2 wt%). Thereafter, 3.3 g of HCl solution (0.5 M), 20.9 g of the prepared CS AA solution and 0.5 g of the CNs-CDs AA suspension were completely mixed. Subsequently, 5.5 mL of GD solution (5 wt%) was immersed. The mixture was kept at 328 K for 2 h to form the hydrogel. The formed hydrogel was washed five times to remove residual reagents and then dried in a vacuum oven at 323 K to a constant weight. The obtained hydrogel was labelled CH-1. Furthermore, 0.8, 1.1, 1.4, 1.7, 2.0, 2.3, 2.6 and 2.9 g of the CNs-CDs AA suspensions were added, respectively. After the same experimental procedure was repeated, the obtained hydrogels were named CH-2, CH-3, CH-4, CH-5, CH-6, CH-7, CH-8 and CH-9. These hydrogels were obtained as long cylindrical rods and then cut into discs of diameter 12 mm and thickness 10–12 mm for use in adsorption experiments.

TC adsorption was studied using blank hydrogel (BH) and CH samples. The BH samples were prepared by the crosslinking reaction of CS and GD. First, 100 mL of the TC solution was transferred into 250 mL conical flasks. Then, about 3.0 g of BH and CH samples were added into separate flasks with continuous shaking. The pH was varied from 2 to 10; the temperature was changed from 288 to 318 K; the contact time was varied from 5 to 160 min, and the initial concentration was changed from 2.5 to 120 mg/L. The effects of these variations on TC adsorption were studied. After adsorption, the TC solutions were filtered, and their concentrations were measured. Adsorption experiments were performed in duplicate, and only the mean values were shown. The maximum deviation for the duplicates was usually less than 5%.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analyses were performed with an S-4800 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was taken on a D/Max-2400 X-Ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured using an IN 10TM FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra were performed on a Lambda 950 UV-Visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected in a K-Alpha 1063 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of CH fabrication. In this work, CNs and CDs were the additional elements. First, the CDs were grafted on the CNs-based skeleton to form CNs-CDs. Second, by stirring vigorously, the CNs-CDs was attached to CS through hydrogen bonding. Finally, the CH was formed by the crosslinking reaction of CS with GD.

Fig. 2 a shows the photographs of a series of CHs with various CDs under daylight and UV light. As the CDs content increased, the fluorescence intensity of the CHs first increased and then decreased. Among these CHs, CH-8 exhibited the strongest fluorescence, and its absolute quantum yield (AQY) was 3.5 %. Meanwhile, the maximum adsorption capacities of these CHs were obtained based on the results of adsorption isotherm experiments (Fig. S3). As shown in this figure, the maximum sorption capacity of CH-8 was higher than that of other CHs. Hence, CH-8 was chosen for further studies.

Fig. 2b-c shows the microstructures of CH-8. CH-8 had a 3D porous structure. This 3D porous structure was built via the crosslinking reaction, and CDs were incorporated into the newly formed structure. The pore characteristics of CH-8 are shown in Fig. S4. The BET surface area, pore volume and average pore diameter of CH-8 were 16.84 m2/g, 0.05621 cm3/g and 7.551 nm, respectively. Its contact angle was measured to be 28.5°, indicating that it was hydrophilic [16,17]. These observations implied that the CH-8 had potential as an effective adsorbent.

Fig. 2d shows the XRD spectra of CS, CNs, CNs-CDs and CH-8. The spectrum of CS showed one broad peak (20.0°) attributed to the amorphous structure [18,19]. The spectra of both CNs and CNs-CDs had two diffraction peaks at 14.9° and 22.7° corresponding to the characteristic inter-plane distances of cellulose I α and I β phases [20]. In the CH-8 spectrum, the peak associated with CS cannot be observed because the CS underwent the crosslinking reaction. Additionally, these two main peaks associated with CNs-CDs disappeared because they were incorporated into CH-8, and thus their crystalline structures were destroyed [21].

The FTIR spectra of CS, CNs, CNs-CDs and CH-8 are shown in Fig. 2e. For CS, the peaks at 3350 and 1550 cm−1 were attributed to –OH stretching vibration and –NH2 bending vibration, respectively. The CNs and CNs-CDs spectra both clearly showed a band at 1050 cm−1 associated with cellulose species [20]. Unlike the CNs spectrum, the CNs-CDs spectrum showed a new band at 1650 cm−1 which was attributed to the stretching vibration of the CONH group [22]. The formation of the CONH group indicated the attachment of CDs on CNs [23]. The spectrum of CH-8 had a characteristic peak at 1647 cm−1 associated with CCNC stretching vibration, possibly implying that CS and GD produced a cross-linked structure [18].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. 2f. The absorption spectrum had one peak at 280 nm associated with the π–π* transition of the CDs [24]. Meanwhile, the maximum excitation wavelength was 370 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength was 464 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of CH-8 with different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 2g. As the excitation wavelengths changed in the range of 325–415 nm, the emission spectra of CH-8 exhibited a gradual red-shift, which was consistent with the results reported in previous studies [25].

In this study, first, the effect of pH on TC sorption was studied. Fig. 3 a shows the change in adsorption capacity with pH. As shown in this figure, the adsorption capacity increased as the pH increased from 2 to 8, and then it decreased as the pH increased further from 8 to 10. The zeta potentials of CH-8 were measured by the streaming potential method (Fig. S5), and the pH of zero charge was about 8.3. Meanwhile, TC has net charges that are positive (pH less than 3.3), neutral (3.3 < pH less than 7.7) and negative (pH > 7.7) depending on the pH [26,27]. At pH 2, the adsorption capacity was low because of the electrostatic repulsion between the positively charged CH-8 and TC. In the pH range of 4–6, TC was neutral, and CH-8 remained positive, resulting in an increase in the adsorption capacity with pH. When the pH reached 8, there was a remarkable increase in the adsorption capacity because of electrostatic attraction. However, as the pH increased further to 10 (TC was negative and CH-8 changed from positive to negative), the electrostatic interaction changed from strong attraction to strong repulsion, which led to the decrease in the adsorption capacity [28]. Thus, the maximum adsorption capacity was observed at pH 8, and the pH was set at this value in the adsorption experiments described.

To evaluate the effect of temperature on the adsorption, adsorption experiments were performed at various temperatures in the range of 288 K–318 K (Fig. 3b). The adsorption capacity of CH-8 increased slightly within the investigated range. These results agreed well with the endothermic nature of the adsorption process [6]. For the purpose of practical application, the room temperature (298 K) was selected.

The initial concentration can also affect TC sorption. Fig. 3c shows the effect of initial concentration. The sorption capacity increased with the initial concentration; the sorption capacity of CH-8 was higher than that of BH at the same initial concentration. Furthermore, the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were used to describe the experimental data. The fitted curves of these two models are shown in Fig. 3e and f, and the corresponding parameters are listed. The figures clearly showed that the Langmuir isotherm model was a better fit to the experimental data with higher correlation coefficient than in the case of the Freundlich isotherm model. This result indicated the occurrence of monolayer sorption of TC on CH-8 [29]. Moreover, the maximum sorption capacity of CH-8 was calculated as 541.3 mg/g. Compared with the other adsorbents (Table 1 ), the CH-8 showed relatively high sorption capacity for TC.

Sorption kinetics is an important factor used to evaluate the properties of the CH. Fig. 3d shows the effect of contact time. The sorption capacity of CH-8 was larger than that of BH, indicating that the sorption rate of CH-8 was higher. Furthermore, the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were used to describe these experimental data. The fitted curves of these two models are plotted in Fig. 3g and h, and the corresponding parameters are listed. The experimental data showed a clearly better fit to the pseudo-second-order model, with higher correlation coefficient than in the case of the pseudo-first-order model. This result indicated that the rate-limiting step for CH-8 was chemical sorption [36].

In addition, regeneration is an important factor for adsorbents. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate the adsorption–desorption cycles of CH-8 (Fig. S6). In this work, these cycles were performed four times. After four cycles, there was only a slight decrease in the sorption capacity (82.0 % of the initial amount after four times), demonstrating the high reusability of CH-8. The slight decrease may be caused by the incomplete desorption of TC in HCl solution (1 mol/L). This result confirmed that CH-8 had good regeneration ability.

To check the selectivity of the CH-8 for TC detection, fluorescence quenching and AQY of the CH-8 in the presence of eight antibiotics (TC, OFX, OTC, CAP, SD, SMZ, CIP and ENR) were investigated (Fig. 4 a and b). The introduction of TC had a strong quenching effect on the fluorescence of CH-8, whereas the other antibiotics had negligible or no effect on the fluorescence of CH-8. Meanwhile, there was an obvious decrease in the AQY of CH-8 only in the presence of TC. Therefore, CH-8 exhibited high selectivity for TC detection.

To check the sensitivity of CH-8 for TC detection, fluorescence quenching and AQY of the CH-8 for different TC concentrations were investigated (Fig. 5 a). With increase in TC concentration, the fluorescence colour of CH-8 changed from cyan to black. Meanwhile, there was a good linear relationship between the AQY of CH-8 and TC concentration in the range of 1–200 mg/L. Fig. 5b shows that the fluorescence intensity of CH-8 at 464 nm decreased with increase in TC concentration. There existed a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.99132) between F0/F–1 and TC concentration. The linear range was from 0.2 to 1.0 μg/L, and the LOD was estimated to be 0.12 μg/L. Both these results were comparable to previous results (Table S1). CH-8 exhibited much better detection performance compared to most of the previously reported materials in terms of both the linear range and LOD.

To evaluate the practical applicability, the detection performance of CH-8 for TC in real water samples was studied. The results are listed in Table S2. No TC was detected in the real tap and lake water samples. Standard solutions containing different concentrations of TC were added to these real water samples for checking the detection accuracy of CH-8. The concentrations detected using CH-8 were close to the actual added concentrations; the recoveries at each concentration ranged from 99.57% to 102.8%, and the relative standard deviations were less than 7.45%. Hence, CH-8 can be potentially used for determining TC in real water samples.

To explore whether the CH was a non-toxic adsorbent, its cytotoxicity against L929 cells in vitro was quantitatively calculated based on cell viability (Fig. 6 a). The cells without the hydrogel were considered as control, and their viability was set as 100%. As the TC concentration was varied from 10 to 500 μg/mL, cell viabilities with CH-8 and BH were around 90%. Even when the concentration reached 500 mg/L, the cell viability exceeded 90%. These results confirmed that CH-8 was a non-toxic adsorbent and that the two additional chemical components i.e. CDs and CNs were non-toxic to L929 cells. The L929 cells embedded in CH-8 were further examined by live–dead staining. Viable (stained blue) and dead cells (stained red) were visualised by fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 6b). A clear majority of cells were viable as confirmed from the ample blue dots filling the whole figure, and very few cells were dead. These results suggested that CH-8 showed good promise as a non-toxic adsorbent for removing TC in water environments.

FTIR spectroscopy was performed to analyse the sorption mechanism of CH. As shown in Fig. 7 a, one obvious peak of NH and OH stretching vibrations at about 3353 cm−1 decreased after TC sorption [37]. Meanwhile, the CN stretching vibration peak at 1450 cm−1 was enhanced [38]. These changes confirmed that the hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups could affect TC sorption.

XPS analysis was performed to characterise the changes in CH-8 before and after TC sorption. The total XPS survey scan (Fig. 7b) shows three characteristic peaks of C1s (284 eV), N1s (399 eV) and O1s (531 eV). Fig. 7c shows the high-resolution XPS results for O1s. Two component peaks at 532.8 and 531.7 eV were assigned to CO and CO, respectively [39]. No new peak was observed after TC sorption, demonstrating that the interaction between COOH and TC was hydrogen bonding. Fig. 7d shows the N1s spectra. The N1s region was split to three component peaks i.e. 398.8, 399.7 and 402.0 eV that were characteristic of N, NH, and N+ [40]. The relative intensity of the N+ peak increased after TC adsorption, which may be attributed to the generation of NH3 + because of the interaction between TC and amino groups. Hence, it was concluded that the electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions worked for the TC sorption.

To elucidate the detection mechanism, the optical properties of TC and CH-8 were studied (Fig. 8 a). TC had a broad UV-vis absorption peak at 357 nm, which was overlapped by the fluorescence excitation spectrum of CH-8. The result confirmed that TC had an inner filter effect on CH-8. Because of the absorption of TC, fewer photons arrived at CH-8, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of CH-8. Moreover, the fluorescence decay curves of CH-8 were obtained before and after TC adsorption (Fig. 8b–d), and the corresponding averages were calculated as 1.37 and 1.33 ns before and after TC adsorption, respectively. The similar lifetimes indicated the interaction may be a static quenching effect [25]. In summary, TC detection was mainly based on the fluorescence quenching of CH-8 because of the generation of ground-state non-fluorescent complexes during TC sorption.

Based on these results, the sorption and detection mechanisms were proposed (Fig. 9 ). The negatively charged TC first accumulated on the surface of the hydrogel because of electric attraction. The 3D porous structures of CH provided numerous transport channels to accelerate the diffusion of TC. With the diffusion of TC into the interior of CH, most of the TC was adsorbed via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. Thus, because of these interactions, CH had strong sorption ability. Meanwhile, some TC was adsorbed on the CDs incorporated into the CH, and the fluorescence quenching of CH because of the static quenching effect was observed clearly. Furthermore, CH had sensitive detection ability for TC.

Cost analysis is crucial in determining the practicality of an adsorption process for water treatment. Generally, the cost of an adsorption process depends mainly on the cost of the adsorbent. Hence, the cost of the prepared hydrogel CH-8 was analysed. According to the cost-estimation procedures reported in literature [41], the breakup cost of each step and the total cost are summarised in Table S3. The total cost for the preparation of 1 kg of CH-8 was estimated as CNY 171.69. Meanwhile, activated carbon (AC) can be purchased from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. (China) at a cost of CNY 253.00 per kg. The cost analysis presented in Table S3 provided simple evidence that CH-8 was less expensive than AC. Furthermore, according to the maximum adsorption capacities of CH-8 (541.3 mg/g) and AC (44.1 mg/g) [42], the costs of these two adsorbents for removing 1 g of TC were calculated (Table S4). As seen from this table, the cost of CH-8 per gram of TC removal was only CNY 0.32 that was very less as compared to the cost of AC i.e. CNY 5.74. CH-8 had a lower cost per gram of TC removal because it had higher adsorption capacity for TC. Hence, considering its environmental and financial benefits, CH-8 was a promising adsorbent for removing TC.

Non-toxic CH based on NCs and CDs was successfully synthesised. The synthesised CH had strong adsorption ability for TC and achieved a maximum sorption capacity of 541.3 mg/g. It had excellent fluorescence, achieving a detection limit of 0.12 μg/L with linear ranges of 0.2–1.0 μg/L and 1–200 mg/L for the selective and sensitive determination of TC. The 3D porous structure of CH was extremely beneficial for TC adsorption and aggregation, enabling the full utilisation of the natural skeleton of CNs and the remarkable fluorescent characteristic of CDs. Hence, the synthesised CH had a strong adsorption ability and sensitive fluorescent response. This work may offer a new approach for the design and preparation of multi-functional chitosan-based hydrogels that can be simultaneously applied for both detection and removal of antibiotics. Development of such a multi-functional hydrogel will be one of the future directions in research on new hydrogels, and newer multi-functional hydrogels are expected to be developed.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Hunan Youth Science and Technology Innovation Talent Project (Grant No. 2020RC3043), Training Plan of Young Backbone Teachers in Colleges and Universities of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 71), Special Projects of Scientific and Technological Innovation in Hunan Forestry, China (Grant No. XLK201982), Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project of Hunan Province (Grant No. 50), and Key Support Areas of Hunan College Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (Grant No. 20).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.131738.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous removal of antibiotics and antibiotic resistance genes from pharmaceutical wastewater using the combinations of up-flow anaerobic sludge bed, anoxic-oxic tank, and advanced oxidation technologies
Water Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Hou, Z. Chen, J. Gao, Y. Xie, L. Li, S. Qin, Q. Wang, D. Mao, Y. Luo, Simultaneous removal of antibiotics and antibiotic resistance genes from pharmaceutical wastewater using the combinations of up-flow anaerobic sludge bed, anoxic-oxic tank, and advanced oxidation technologies, Water Res. 159 (2019), 511-520.
